raw_case:
Hyperinsulinemic Hypoglycemia in a Patient With a Mutation in the Insulin Receptor


Abstract

Hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemias resulting from variants in the insulin receptor (INSR) gene are rare but clinically important disorders. We present a male patient in his 30s, experiencing recurrent postprandial hypoglycemic events. Endocrine evaluation revealed an elevated insulin-to-C-peptide ratio. A hypoglycemia gene panel, using next-generation sequencing, identified a heterozygous nonsense variant in the INSR gene (NM_000208.4) c.3079C > T, p.(Arg1027*). Initial treatment with diazoxide reduced hypoglycemic symptoms and led to weight loss and decreased hemoglobin A1c due to reduced compensatory carbohydrate intake. However, limiting side effects on diazoxide prompted a treatment switch to lanreotide with maintained absence of hypoglycemic events. This case highlights the importance of considering variants in the INSR gene as a differential diagnosis in hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia cases, even in adults.

Introduction

Although there is no universally accepted definition of hypoglycemia, it is often defined as plasma glucose level below 70 mg/dL (3.9 mmol/L) with concurrent adrenergic symptoms that include, but are not limited to, shakiness, irritability, tachycardia, sweating, and hunger. When plasma glucose level falls below 54 mg/dL (3.0 mmol/L), neuroglycopenic symptoms such as confusion, dizziness, behavioral changes, diplopia, seizures, and somnolence may occur [1]. Hypoglycemia in nondiabetic adults is uncommon. Endogenous hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia is a condition characterized by dysregulated insulin secretion and/or clearance, leading to recurrent episodes of hypoglycemia [2]. The etiologies range from inborn errors of metabolism to acquired conditions including insulinoma and postbariatric surgery. Gene variants in the insulin receptor gene (INSR) represent a rare yet clinically relevant cause of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia. The insulin receptor plays a pivotal role in mediating the biological effects of insulin [3]. It is a transmembrane glycoprotein primarily expressed in insulin-responsive tissues such as skeletal muscle, adipose tissue, and liver. Activation of the insulin receptor initiates a cascade of intracellular signaling events that regulate glucose uptake, glycogen synthesis, lipid metabolism, and cell growth. Variants in the INSR gene can impair normal insulin receptor function and result in insulin resistance of varying severity. However, there are also a few reported cases of variants in the INSR gene associated with hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia [4-8].Our patient was diagnosed in adulthood with hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia following a history of hypoglycemic symptoms since childhood.

Case Presentation

A 33-year-old male patient was referred to the endocrine outpatient clinic because of suspected hypoglycemic events. Originally from southwestern Europe, he relocated to Sweden 9 years prior to the first visit. He had a history of allergic rhinitis from pollen, asthma, and diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D). His medication list included amitriptyline 20 mg at bedtime, loratadine 10 mg as needed, loperamide 2 mg as needed, psyllium daily, budesonide inhalation 100 μg twice per day, and terbutaline 200 μg inhalation as needed. He was not taking any supplements. He presented to his general practitioner with a history of recurrent postprandial symptoms of dizziness, diaphoresis, pallor, and fatigue several times weekly. He had acquired a standard blood glucose meter and observed that his symptoms coincided with capillary blood glucose levels between 45 and 49 mg/dL (2.5-2.7 mmol/L), with occasional levels even lower alongside presyncope. His symptoms typically started 2 to 4 hours postprandially, and never during fasting. The symptoms also presented during physical exercise and occasionally even when walking his dog. His symptoms were relieved upon consuming carbohydrates, which was paralleled with normalized capillary glucose levels, thereby meeting the criteria of the Whipple triad [2].At presentation, he had no signs of acanthosis nigricans. His weight was 198 lb (90 kg) with a body mass index (BMI) of 33 kg/m2. A thorough medical history revealed recurrent similar events since childhood, initially a few times a year, gradually increasing to once or twice per month during adolescence. The frequency of the symptoms had increased over the past 5 years and was now significantly impacting his quality of life. Notably, his father, paternal uncle, and cousin experienced similar symptoms.

Diagnostic Assessment

The diagnostic process was initiated with repeated fasting venous blood samples showing plasma glucose levels between 85 and 94 mg/dL (4.7-5.2 mmol/L) (reference range: 76-114 mg/dL; 4.2-6.3 mmol/L) with simultaneous normal plasma C-peptide levels of 2.8 to 3.0 ng/mL (.94-1.00 nmol/L) (reference range: 1.1-4.4 ng/mL; .37-1.47 nmol/L) but notably increased plasma insulin levels at 50 to 58 mIU/L (347-403 pmol/L) (reference range: 2.6-24.9 mIU/L; 35-174 pmol/L) and plasma proinsulin 23 to 33 pmol/L (reference range: 3.3-28 pmol/L) (Fig. 1). Hence, the insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio was increased at .35 to .40 (normally <.1) [4]. Morning plasma cortisol was 12.1 µg/dL (335 nmol/L), serum IGF-1 was at 153 ng/mL (20.0 nmol/L) (reference range 73-246 ng/mL; 9.5-32.1 nmol/L) and ß-hydroxybutyrate was undetectable. Considering the history of IBS-D, laboratory testing for possible neuroendocrine tumor was conducted and results were unremarkable (Table 1).He was admitted to the medical ward for a 72-hour fasting test. After a carbohydrate-rich breakfast, fasting started at 10 Am and ended prematurely after 49 hours because of intolerable tremor and diaphoresis, promptly relieved after intake of carbohydrates. At termination of fasting, his plasma glucose level was 52 mg/dL (2.9 mmol/L) and plasma insulin level was 9.2 mIU/L (64 pmol/L). The insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio decreased after 24 hours of fasting but was still notably elevated at .21 (Table 2).Genetic investigation with a hypoglycemia gene panel using next-generation sequencing identified a heterozygous INSR nonsense variant (NM_000208.4) c.3079C > T, p.(Arg1027*). The variant was considered as pathogenic according to current American College of Medical Genetics criteria [9]. It has previously been described in heterozygous form associated with mild insulin resistance and has been shown to result in decreased mRNA levels [10, 11].Our patient was provided with genetic counseling, including written and oral information about his genetic results. As the patient's relatives were residing outside of Sweden, further family investigation was not possible at our center. However, the patient was informed of the possibility for his relatives to pursue genetic counseling and investigation in their country of residence. The patient contacted his relatives, but none opted to undergo genetic testing. He has no biological children.

Treatment

Our patient returned for follow-up 5 months later at the endocrine outpatient clinic and was now experiencing almost daily symptoms. His medications were reviewed again. He had initiated and discontinued sertraline 50 mg every day and mirtazapine 15 mg every day during the year of diagnostic assessment due to a depressive episode. He had discontinued his inhalations and started topical lidocaine ointment due to an anal fissure. No apparent triggers for increased hypoglycemic events were identified. He had tried several different diets, on his own initiative, without improvement. He had started compensating for the hypoglycemic episodes by eating excessively with subsequent weight gain and was now weighing 204 to 208 lb (92.5-94.5 kg) and BMI 34 to 35 kg/m2. He became extremely fatigued by the hypoglycemic events and was seeking a treatment option.He was started on diazoxide 25 mg in the morning and received a continuous glucose monitor. Two weeks later, he reported fewer hypoglycemic events, with remaining episodes generally occurring in the evenings. Glucose monitoring indicated daytime levels ranging from 79 to 139 mg/dL (4.4-7.7 mmol/L) with a drop to 66 to 68 mg/dL (3.7-3.8 mmol/L) around 6 Pm, accompanied by reported symptoms. He was experiencing some side effects from diazoxide, mainly tachycardia, which restricted his ability to exercise. To further alleviate his hypoglycemic symptoms, the dose was increased by an additional 25 mg in the afternoon. He reported an initial paradoxical increase in hypoglycemic events for the first week, followed by no episodes the following week. The tachycardia persisted, prompting him to switch his physical exercise from cardio to weightlifting.

Outcome and Follow-up

He was followed with regular laboratory assessments. During the first 6 months of treatment, glucose monitoring showed almost no glucose level below 70 mg/dL (3.9 mmol/L) and he reported very few hypoglycemic symptoms. His IBS-D symptoms seemed to improve, and he was able to discontinue the daily psyllium use. Notably, he also lost weight (−10 kg) and his hemoglobin A1c decreased from 5.9% to 5.4% (from 41 to 36 mmol/mol) (reference range: 4.6%-6.0%; 27-42 mmol/mol). After 6 months, he was still experiencing constraining tachycardia, especially during exercise. He was prescribed metoprolol 25 to 50 mg, which provided some relief. A 24-hour Holter monitoring showed sinus tachycardia 11% of the registered time.Ten months after initiating diazoxide, he had a short period where he started experiencing increased episodes of nocturnal hypoglycemia with glucose levels as low as 49 mg/dL (2.7 mmol/L). He temporarily added another 25 mg of diazoxide in the evening. The nocturnal hypoglycemia immediately ceased and a few days later he was able to return to his previous dose.After consulting Professor Kurt Højlund, University of Southern Denmark, Odense University Hospital, who was managing a Danish family with a variant in the INSR gene causing similar postprandial hypoglycemic episodes, the treatment was switched from diazoxide to lanreotide 60 mg every 4 weeks [4]. The side effects quickly subsided with maintained euglycemia. Our patient was able to start exercising cardio again and resume an ordinary lifestyle.During the first year of hypoglycemic treatment, his weight continued to gradually decrease. At the latest follow-up, 17 months after initiation of diazoxide, bodyweight was 141 lb (64 kg) and BMI 24 kg/m2.

Discussion

We present a case of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia presenting in adulthood, in a patient carrying a heterozygous variant in the INSR gene, which results in a premature stop codon and reduced mRNA levels, which is expected to lead to a decrease of available functioning insulin receptors. The variant has previously been described to cause insulin resistance in heterozygous form, which is rather the opposite of our patient's phenotype of hypoglycemic events. However, there is support for a role for the insulin receptor, not only in insulin signaling, but also in hepatic insulin clearance [3, 12]. This implies that an INSR gene variant leading to deficient, or functionally impaired, insulin receptors could potentially alter both peripheral insulin signaling and hepatic insulin clearance. Depending on the relative extents of these 2 effects, a variant in the INSR gene could possibly result in decreased insulin clearance, leading to increased levels of endogenous circulating insulin postprandially and thereby be causative of hypoglycemia. In our case, functional studies of the INSR nonsense variant would be required to ascertain this hypothesis, but its plausibility is underlined by previous reports of missense variants in the INSR gene being associated with hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia [4-8]. Among these, the variant p.(Arg1174Gln) has been reported with a phenotype similar to our patient's phenotype [4]. In this family described by Højlund et al, the phenotype of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia was observed in association with an increased insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio >.1, congruent with the consistently elevated insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio of .35 to .40 seen in our patient (Fig. 1). Previous reports of patients with variants in the INSR gene and similar phenotypes have included oral glucose tolerance testing, showing excessive increases in plasma insulin levels [4-8]. Although our patient never underwent a conventional 75-g oral glucose tolerance test, we did see similar excessive insulin levels directly after the breakfast preceding the 72-hour fasting test with a plasma insulin level at 348 mIU/L (2417 pmol/L) (Table 2).The 72-hour fasting test ended prematurely because of hypoglycemia, and the plasma insulin levels simultaneously increased slightly from 6.2 mIU/L (43 pmol/L) to 9.2 mIU/L (64 pmol/L), proposing a possible insulinoma. However, our patient had no clinical signs indicative of this, with symptoms since childhood, no history of hypoglycemia during fasting, no significantly low blood glucose levels (<2.5 mmol/L) during the test, and no impressively increased insulin-to-glucose ratio. We therefore considered insulinoma to be unlikely. The median biological coefficient of variation of plasma insulin is approximately 25% and we considered this to be the most probable cause of the slight increase [13]. We noted our patient was taking a once-daily low dose of amitriptyline to alleviate symptoms of IBS-D. Tricyclic antidepressants have previously been described in 2 cases in a case report to cause hypoglycemia in the setting of concomitant sulfonylurea use [14]. However, the patients in these cases were taking higher doses of tricyclic antidepressants and, looking retrospectively, we could not see any temporal association between our patient's long-term fixed dose of 20 mg at bedtime and the gradual increase in hypoglycemic events over many years. Consequently, we consider a link between patient's use of amitriptyline use and the hypoglycemic events to be unlikely.Previously described cases have presented with a family history with a variety of clinical manifestations, spanning from hypoglycemia to insulin resistance, the latter more commonly found in the elderly [4, 5, 7]. For our patient, family studies were not possible as none of the patient's family members chose to pursue genetic testing. Because several other family members have been described to have experienced similar symptoms, family studies would have been valuable to further investigate the causality of the detected variant in the INSR gene. Considering the phenotypical spectrum of INSR gene variants and the consistently increased insulin levels, our patient will be closely monitored for the potential development of type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Learning Points

Variants in the INSR gene are rare yet clinically relevant causes of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia.Variants in the INSR gene should be considered as a differential diagnosis in patients presenting with postprandial hypoglycemia, even in adults.Continuous glucose monitoring is a valuable tool in the treatment and management of hypoglycemia.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 33-year-old male, originally from southwestern Europe, residing in Sweden.
- **Chief Complaint:** Recurrent postprandial hypoglycemic symptoms (dizziness, diaphoresis, pallor, fatigue, occasional presyncope).
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptoms started since childhood, initially infrequent but increased in severity and frequency over time. Symptoms are consistently relieved by consuming carbohydrates, aligning with Whipple's triad. No hypoglycemic episodes during fasting until recent fasting test.
- **Past Medical History:** Allergic rhinitis, asthma, diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D).
- **Personal History:** Taking medications including amitriptyline, loratadine, loperamide, psyllium, inhaled budesonide, and terbutaline. No supplement use.
- **Family History:** Paternal family members (father, uncle, cousin) with similar symptoms. No genetic testing completed among relatives.
- **Physical Examination:** No acanthosis nigricans; weight increased to BMI 34-35 kg/m² during hypoglycemia treatment.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Plasma glucose during fasting: 85-94 mg/dL, with high plasma insulin (50-58 mIU/L) and insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio (>0.35).
  - Plasma glucose during fasting test dropped to 52 mg/dL with insulin at 9.2 mIU/L; high insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio (0.21).
  - Genetic testing: Heterozygous pathogenic nonsense variant in INSR gene (c.3079C>T, p.Arg1027*).
  - Negative tests for neuroendocrine tumors and normal β-hydroxybutyrate levels.
  - Continuous glucose monitoring: symptomatic hypoglycemia postprandially, almost daily.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - **Common causes of hypoglycemia:** Insulinoma, factitious hypoglycemia, medication-induced hypoglycemia, inborn errors of metabolism.
   - **Uncommon genetic conditions:** Variants in the INSR gene.
2. **Exclusion of Insulinoma:** 
   - Symptoms since childhood, no profound fasting hypoglycemia (<2.5 mmol/L) or significantly elevated insulin-to-glucose ratio during the fasting test, making insulinoma unlikely.
3. **Exclusion of Factitious Hypoglycemia:**
   - Elevated endogenous insulin levels and absence of exogenous insulin or sulfonylurea use proved this unlikely.
4. **Connection to INSR Mutation:**
   - Detection of heterozygous INSR nonsense variant with high insulin-to-C-peptide ratio postprandially suggested impaired insulin clearance due to altered receptor function. This aligns with prior reported cases.
5. **Exclusion of Medication-Induced Hypoglycemia:**
   - Long-term, fixed-dose, low-dose amitriptyline unlikely to be causative given no temporal association.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia due to a heterozygous nonsense mutation in the **INSR gene**. This mutation decreases functioning insulin receptor availability, impairing hepatic insulin clearance and causing elevated circulating insulin levels postprandially.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia secondary to heterozygous INSR nonsense mutation.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start analyzing this case. The patient is a 33-year-old male with recurrent postprandial hypoglycemia. His symptoms started in childhood and have worsened over time. The key here is that the episodes happen after eating, not during fasting, which initially points away from insulinoma but maybe towards something like reactive hypoglycemia. But wait, the recent fasting test did show a drop in glucose to 52 mg/dL, which complicates things. However, during that test, his insulin was 9.2 mIU/L. Let me recall the insulin levels in different conditions. In insulinoma, you'd expect inappropriately high insulin during fasting. But here, during the fasting test, insulin was 9.2 when glucose was low. I need to check the insulin/glucose ratio. At 52 mg/dL (which is about 2.9 mmol/L), the insulin should be suppressed. If insulin is still detectable, that's abnormal. The insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio is high here (0.21). Wait, normal ratio is less than 0.1, I think. So that's elevated. High insulin with low C-peptide suggests exogenous insulin use or maybe an insulinoma, but the tests for neuroendocrine tumors were negative. But the patient isn't on insulin, so that's ruled out.

Then there's the genetic testing: heterozygous pathogenic variant in the INSR gene. INSR mutations are linked to insulin resistance syndromes like Rabson-Mendenhall syndrome or type A insulin resistance. But wait, how does that connect to hypoglycemia? Wait, insulin receptor mutations can sometimes cause paradoxical hypoglycemia. Because in some cases, especially with certain mutations, there might be postprandial hypoglycemia due to altered insulin receptor signaling. For example, in insulin receptor mutations leading to hyperinsulinemia, the body might oversecrete insulin because the receptors aren't working properly, leading to delayed clearance and then hypoglycemia. But I'm a bit confused here. Alternatively, maybe the mutation is causing a gain-of-function, but the variant mentioned is a nonsense mutation, which would typically lead to a loss of function. A loss-of-function mutation in the insulin receptor would cause insulin resistance, leading to hyperglycemia, but the patient has hypoglycemia. Hmm, conflicting information here.

Wait, maybe the heterozygous mutation leads to a dominant negative effect. Let me think. The INSR gene codes for the insulin receptor. If there's a nonsense mutation leading to a truncated protein, it might interfere with the normal receptors, leading to decreased receptor activity. That would result in insulin resistance. But insulin resistance usually leads to hyperglycemia and type 2 diabetes. However, in some cases, particularly in certain tissues, impaired insulin signaling might lead to compensatory hyperinsulinemia. But how does that result in hypoglycemia? Or perhaps there's a different mechanism. Wait, there's a condition called hypoglycemia due to insulin receptor mutations, like in the case of mutations that cause a post-receptor defect leading to inappropriate insulin secretion. For instance, in some cases, the mutation might cause the insulin receptor to be hyperactive in certain cells, leading to increased glucose uptake in some tissues but resistance in others. But this is getting complicated.

Wait, the patient's lab during fasting shows high plasma insulin (50-58 mIU/L) with normal glucose (85-94). High insulin with normal glucose suggests insulin resistance. But in the fasting state, insulin should be low. So if insulin is high but glucose is normal, that implies the body is producing more insulin to maintain glucose levels, indicating resistance. But then during the fasting test, when glucose dropped to 52, insulin was 9.2. Normally, when glucose is low, insulin should be suppressed. If insulin is 9.2, which is not extremely high, but if glucose is 52, that's inappropriately normal or slightly elevated. So the insulin/glucose ratio here would be 9.2 /52. Let me convert glucose to mmol/L to check. 52 mg/dL is ~2.9 mmol/L. The insulin (9.2 mIU/L) in SI units is about 66 pmol/L (since 1 mIU/L is ~7 pmol/L). So insulin of 66 pmol/L with glucose 2.9 mmol/L. The critical sample for hypoglycemia: insulin should be <3 mIU/L (or <18 pmol/L) when glucose is <3 mmol/L. Here, insulin is higher than that, so that's inappropriately elevated. So this suggests endogenous hyperinsulinemia. But the C-peptide level? The molar ratio of insulin to C-peptide was 0.21. Normally, when insulin is exogenous, C-peptide is low (since exogenous insulin suppresses endogenous production), so the ratio would be high. But here, if both insulin and C-peptide are elevated, the ratio would be around 1. If the ratio is high (e.g., insulin is high but C-peptide is low), that suggests exogenous insulin. But in this case, the ratio was 0.21, which is higher than the normal cutoff (I think normal is <0.1). Wait, but during the fasting test, insulin was 9.2 and C-peptide was... not given. Wait, the molar ratio is calculated as insulin (mIU/L) divided by C-peptide (ng/mL). Wait, the units might be different. Let me clarify. The molar ratio is (insulin in pmol/L) divided by (C-peptide in pmol/L). Because 1 mIU/L of insulin is approximately 6 pmol/L, and C-peptide is measured in ng/mL, which converts to pmol/L by multiplying by 333 (since 1 ng/mL = 0.333 nmol/L = 333 pmol/L). But maybe the lab uses a different conversion. Alternatively, the insulin (mIU/L) to C-peptide (ng/mL) ratio. If the ratio is >0.3, that's considered suggestive of exogenous insulin. Wait, the first test had a ratio >0.35. The fasting test had a ratio of 0.21. So in the initial test, high ratio, but during fasting test, lower but still above 0.1. This might indicate that in the initial test, when glucose was normal, insulin is high and C-peptide is not as high, leading to a higher ratio. But in the fasting test, insulin is lower but C-peptide may also be lower. Wait, but if endogenous insulin is being produced, both insulin and C-peptide should be elevated. If exogenous insulin is given, C-peptide would be low. So a high ratio (insulin/C-peptide) would suggest exogenous insulin. But in this patient, during the fasting test, ratio is 0.21. If normal ratio is around 0.03 to 0.1, then 0.21 is higher. But not as high as in exogenous insulin. So this might indicate endogenous hyperinsulinemia but with some suppression of C-peptide. Wait, this is getting confusing. But the genetic test shows a pathogenic INSR variant, which complicates things.

The other important point is the family history. His paternal relatives have similar symptoms, suggesting an autosomal dominant condition. The INSR mutation is heterozygous, which fits. So the INSR mutation is pathogenic, leading to some form of insulin receptor dysfunction. But how does that cause hypoglycemia?

Wait, there's a condition called type B insulin resistance, which is due to autoantibodies against the insulin receptor. But that's an autoimmune condition. The patient here has a genetic mutation, not autoantibodies. Another possibility is that the mutation in INSR leads to a defective receptor, causing insulin resistance. However, some mutations can lead to a paradoxical effect. For example, in Donohue syndrome (leprechaunism), which is caused by homozygous INSR mutations, there is severe insulin resistance, but sometimes episodes of hypoglycemia. However, this patient is an adult, so Donohue is unlikely. Another condition is Rabson-Mendenhall syndrome, also due to INSR mutations, which includes insulin resistance, but again, severe and presents in childhood. The patient's variant is a heterozygous nonsense mutation. Since INSR is on chromosome 19, and if the mutation is dominant negative, then even one copy can cause significant issues.

But how does insulin receptor dysfunction lead to hypoglycemia? One possible mechanism is that in the liver, insulin resistance leads to reduced suppression of glucose production, but in muscle or adipose tissue, resistance leads to hyperinsulinemia. However, the body might compensate by oversecreting insulin, leading to postprandial hyperinsulinemia. After a meal, insulin is released, but because of resistance, more is needed. However, if there's a delay in glucose uptake or processing, it might lead to an overproduction of insulin which then causes a late drop in glucose levels, resulting in reactive hypoglycemia. Alternatively, perhaps the mutation affects the insulin receptor in a way that causes intermittent overactivity in certain tissues, leading to excessive glucose uptake after meals, causing hypoglycemia.

Another angle: the high insulin levels during fasting (when glucose is normal) suggest insulin resistance. But during fasting, the body should not be secreting much insulin. However, in insulin resistance, beta cells hypersecrete insulin to overcome the resistance. So in the fasting state, the patient's insulin is high (50-58 mIU/L), which is way above normal (fasting insulin is usually <25 mIU/L, but varies). High insulin with normal glucose indicates insulin resistance. Then during the fasting test, glucose dropped to 52, but insulin was 9.2. This suggests that when glucose falls, insulin is not appropriately suppressed. Normally, when glucose drops below 70 mg/dL, insulin should be undetectable or very low. So 9.2 is still detectable, which is inappropriate. This suggests endogenous hyperinsulinemia. But why?

The genetic mutation in INSR might be causing a defect where the beta cells are not properly regulated. Wait, insulin receptors are present on beta cells and play a role in insulin secretion. If the insulin receptor is defective, maybe the beta cells don't get the feedback signal to stop secreting insulin. For example, normally, insulin binds to its receptor on beta cells and inhibits further secretion (a negative feedback). If the receptor is non-functional, this inhibition doesn't occur, leading to unregulated insulin secretion. This could cause hyperinsulinemia, leading to hypoglycemia. That makes sense. So in this case, the INSR mutation in beta cells disrupts the normal feedback mechanism, causing excessive insulin secretion despite low glucose levels. This would lead to postprandial hypoglycemia because after eating, insulin is released, but the beta cells can't turn off secretion properly. This fits with the patient's symptoms starting after meals. The high insulin during fasting may be part of this dysregulation. Additionally, the family history of similar symptoms supports an inherited mutation in the INSR gene causing this condition.

The other tests ruled out insulinoma (negative for neuroendocrine tumors) and factitious hypoglycemia (high insulin-to-C-peptide ratio, but not as high as in exogenous insulin use). The genetic finding of a pathogenic INSR variant confirms the diagnosis. The condition here is likely a form of congenital hyperinsulinemia due to an INSR mutation. Wait, but congenital hyperinsulinemia is typically caused by mutations in genes like ABCC8 or KCNJ11, which are involved in the KATP channel of beta cells. However, there are other genetic causes, including mutations in INSR. Wait, in literature, mutations in the insulin receptor can cause hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia. For example, certain dominant-negative mutations in INSR have been reported to cause hypoglycemia due to dysregulated insulin secretion. Specifically, some mutations lead to impaired receptor function in pancreatic beta cells, disrupting the normal feedback inhibition of insulin secretion. This results in inappropriate insulin release even when blood glucose is low, leading to hypoglycemia. So this fits the patient's presentation.

Therefore, the final diagnosis is hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia caused by a pathogenic mutation in the INSR gene. This condition might be referred to as "insulin receptor-related hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia" or part of the spectrum of genetic hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia. The family history supports an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, given the paternal relatives with similar symptoms. The recurrent postprandial episodes, elevated insulin levels despite low glucose, and genetic confirmation all point to this diagnosis.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with recurrent postprandial hypoglycemia fulfilling Whipple’s triad, suggesting endogenous hyperinsulinemia. The absence of fasting hypoglycemia initially argues against insulinoma but aligns with reactive hypoglycemia. However, the recent fasting test revealed hypoglycemia (52 mg/dL) with inappropriately detectable insulin (9.2 mIU/L) and a high insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio (0.21), indicating dysregulated insulin secretion.

<step 2> The genetic testing identified a heterozygous pathogenic nonsense variant in the INSR gene (c.3079C>T, p.Arg1027*). INSR mutations typically cause insulin resistance syndromes (e.g., Rabson-Mendenhall), but this variant may disrupt insulin receptor signaling in beta cells, impairing feedback inhibition of insulin secretion. This leads to unregulated insulin release, driving hypoglycemia despite low glucose levels.

<step 3> The family history of paternal relatives with similar symptoms supports an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Negative neuroendocrine tumor testing and normal β-hydroxybutyrate levels exclude insulinoma and ketotic hypoglycemia. Elevated fasting insulin (50-58 mIU/L) with normoglycemia reflects compensatory hyperinsulinemia due to insulin resistance, but postprandial hypoglycemia dominates due to beta-cell dysregulation.

<step 4> The combination of genetic evidence, biochemical findings, postprandial hypoglycemia pattern, and familial clustering confirms a diagnosis of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia secondary to the INSR mutation. This represents a rare form of congenital hyperinsulinemia linked to insulin receptor dysfunction.

### Answer:
Hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia due to a pathogenic heterozygous INSR gene variant (c.3079C>T, p.Arg1027*).

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with recurrent postprandial hypoglycemia fulfilling Whipple’s triad, suggesting endogenous hyperinsulinemia. The absence of fasting hypoglycemia initially argues against insulinoma but aligns with reactive hypoglycemia. However, the recent fasting test revealed hypoglycemia (52 mg/dL) with inappropriately detectable insulin (9.2 mIU/L) and a high insulin-to-C-peptide molar ratio (0.21), indicating dysregulated insulin secretion.

The genetic testing identified a heterozygous pathogenic nonsense variant in the INSR gene (c.3079C>T, p.Arg1027*). INSR mutations typically cause insulin resistance syndromes (e.g., Rabson-Mendenhall), but this variant may disrupt insulin receptor signaling in beta cells, impairing feedback inhibition of insulin secretion. This leads to unregulated insulin release, driving hypoglycemia despite low glucose levels.

The family history of paternal relatives with similar symptoms supports an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Negative neuroendocrine tumor testing and normal β-hydroxybutyrate levels exclude insulinoma and ketotic hypoglycemia. Elevated fasting insulin (50-58 mIU/L) with normoglycemia reflects compensatory hyperinsulinemia due to insulin resistance, but postprandial hypoglycemia dominates due to beta-cell dysregulation.

The combination of genetic evidence, biochemical findings, postprandial hypoglycemia pattern, and familial clustering confirms a diagnosis of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia secondary to the INSR mutation. This represents a rare form of congenital hyperinsulinemia linked to insulin receptor dysfunction.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Identify common causes of hypoglycemia including insulinoma, factitious hypoglycemia, medication-induced hypoglycemia, and inborn errors of metabolism.
Step 2: Exclude insulinoma based on symptoms since childhood and lack of profound fasting hypoglycemia or significantly elevated insulin-to-glucose ratio during the fasting test.
Step 3: Exclude factitious hypoglycemia due to elevated endogenous insulin levels and absence of exogenous insulin or sulfonylurea use.
Step 4: Suggest impaired insulin clearance due to an INSR nonsense variant with a high insulin-to-C-peptide ratio postprandially, aligning with prior reported cases.
Step 5: Exclude medication-induced hypoglycemia as long-term, fixed-dose, low-dose amitriptyline use is unlikely to be causative without a temporal association.
Step 6: Diagnose hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia due to a heterozygous nonsense mutation in the INSR gene.
Step 7: Explain that this mutation decreases functioning insulin receptor availability, impairing hepatic insulin clearance and causing elevated circulating insulin levels postprandially.
